body.title: [triage] memory_emit.emit() silently SHREDS a string namespace into characters — 6 of 6 session records in the store are corrupt. A string and a list of segments are both iterables of strings, so the write path cannot tell them apart.
body.intention: I intend that the memory store's write path REFUSES a malformed record rather than writing a corrupt one — because an interface may collapse an error into a success shape, but a RECORD OF TRUTH may not: an interface can be re-called, a record cannot be un-written. [intention_date: 2026-07-14]
body.description:
**MEASURED 2026-07-14, while starting at the bottom of the stack (Akien's direction).**

`devlab/claudecode/memory_emit.py:162` —

    ns = [s for s in (namespace or []) if s]

`emit()` means `namespace` to be a LIST OF SEGMENTS. The CLI honors that (line 227 splits a dotted
string on [.,] first). But a **Python caller passing a plain string** has it iterated CHARACTER BY
CHARACTER — because a string IS an iterable of one-character strings. The comprehension cannot
distinguish `["session", "20260713"]` from `"session-20260713"`, so it does not fail. It writes a
corrupt record and returns success.

**THE CALLER:** `scripts/session_capture.py:153`

    memory_emit.emit("sessions", "cc.0", body, kind="session", namespace=f"session-{datestamp}")

**THE DAMAGE, counted:**

    sessions     ok=  0   char-split=  6     <-- 100% CORRUPT
    decisions    ok=278   char-split=  0
    tickets      ok=2388  char-split=  0
    proofs       ok=174   char-split=  0
    notes        ok= 46   char-split=  0
    rules        ok= 22   char-split=  0
    architecture ok= 24   char-split=  0

Every session record ever written is corrupt. On disk:

    namespace: ['s','e','s','s','i','o','n','-','2','0','2','6','0','7','1','3']
    filename : cc.0.s.e.s.s.i.o.n.-.2.0.2.6.0.7.1.3.20260712.210210576003.json

The store's whole value is that it is GREP-ABLE. A session is now findable by neither its namespace
nor its filename. Blast radius is sessions today only because every other category goes through the
CLI — but **the defect is in emit(), and it will shred the next Python caller too.**

**THIS IS THE DAY'S RECURRING DEFECT, ONE LAYER FURTHER DOWN: a cause->signal map that is not
injective.** Two different inputs (a segment list, a bare string) produce one indistinguishable shape
(an iterable of strings), so no discriminator exists and the error collapses into a success. Same
shape as crash-vs-CP1-refusal, reachability-vs-capacity, INDETERMINATE-vs-GREEN, and
no-counts-vs-zero-failures. CLAUDE.md already names the rule this violates: *an error may collapse
into a success shape at an INTERFACE, never into a RECORD OF TRUTH.*

**Affected files:** devlab/claudecode/memory_emit.py (emit); scripts/session_capture.py:153;
the 6 corrupt records in devlab/runtime/memory/sessions/.

**Scope boundary:** fix the write path and repair the 6 records. Do NOT redesign the namespace scheme.

**Completion criteria:** (1) emit() RAISES TypeError on a str namespace, naming the fix ('pass a list of
segments, or a dotted string via the CLI') — it must not helpfully coerce, because a helpful coercion is
how this got in; (2) session_capture.py passes a list; (3) the 6 corrupt records are repaired IN PLACE
(annotate, never delete — CP2), namespace and filename both; (4) sessions are grep-able by namespace again.

**Test plan:** red->green. RED: `emit(..., namespace='session-20260713')` currently writes
`['s','e','s',...]`; the test asserts it raises. GREEN: it raises, and a list namespace still round-trips.
Second node: no record in the store has a namespace that is a list of 1-char strings. The hollow build
this must fail: coercing a str to [str] and calling it fixed — that hides the caller's bug instead of
surfacing it, and the next caller passing a dotted string gets a single wrong segment.
